item.title: Claude Code
item.description:
Claude Code is Anthropic’s agentic coding tool. It operates as an interactive agent that reads and searches codebases, edits files, executes shell commands, manages git workflows, and runs tests — using Claude models to reason about each step. Unlike chat-based code assistants that suggest snippets for a human to paste, Claude Code acts directly on the filesystem and shell, making it an agent rather than an advisor.

Claude Code is available as a CLI (the claude command, installed via npm), a VS Code extension, a JetBrains extension, a desktop app (Mac and Windows), and a web app (claude.ai/code). All interfaces run the same underlying agent engine. See IDE integration for the editor extensions.

item.title: Hooks
item.description:
Hooks are shell commands that Claude Code executes at defined points in the tool-call lifecycle. They run deterministically, outside the model’s reasoning loop — the model does not decide whether to run a hook, cannot suppress a hook, and receives hook output as user-level feedback. This makes hooks the appropriate mechanism for safety guardrails, compliance logging, and automated workflows that must execute regardless of what the model decides.

The contrast with CLAUDE.md is definitional: CLAUDE.md shapes model reasoning (advisory, probabilistic), while hooks execute shell commands on events (deterministic, mandatory).

item.title: IDE Integration
item.description:
Claude Code provides native extensions for VS Code (including Cursor and Windsurf) and JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, and other IntelliJ-family editors). The extensions embed the same agent engine as the CLI directly in the editor, adding a graphical layer for code review and navigation.

The VS Code extension provides:

  • Inline diff review — proposed edits appear as diffs in the editor; the user accepts or rejects each change before it is applied.
  • Auto-accept mode — edits apply as they are made, for users who prefer post-hoc review.
  • File mentions — @-mention files with specific line ranges from the current selection to direct Claude’s attention to particular code.
  • Conversation history — access and resume previous sessions.
  • Multi-tab conversations — multiple Claude sessions in separate tabs or editor panes.
  • Checkpoints — the extension tracks Claude’s file edits and allows rewinding to any previous state via a rewind button on each message.
  • Status line — a configurable status bar showing the agent’s current activity.

Installation: open the Extensions view (Ctrl+Shift+X on Windows/Linux, Cmd+Shift+X on Mac), search “Claude Code”, click Install.

item.title: Permission Model
item.description:
The permission model is the set of rules governing which tool calls Claude Code executes automatically and which require human approval before proceeding. It is the primary mechanism for balancing agent autonomy against safety in agentic coding.

Claude Code categorizes actions by reversibility and blast radius. Local, reversible actions — reading files, searching code, writing to local files — default to automatic execution. Destructive or externally visible actions — force-pushing to a remote, deleting files, posting to APIs, sending messages to external services — default to requiring confirmation. The user sees the proposed action and must approve or deny it before the runtime executes the tool call.

item.title: Slash Commands and Skills
item.description:
Slash commands are Claude Code’s mechanism for invoking task-specific instructions on demand. A user types /name at the prompt to load a command’s instructions into the conversation context. Claude Code ships with built-in commands (/help, /clear, /compact, /context, /review) that perform fixed operations, and supports user-defined skills backed by SKILL.md files.

A skill is a slash command with its own instruction document. Each SKILL.md has YAML frontmatter (name, description) and markdown body (the instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked). The name field becomes the /slash-command. The description field serves double duty: it labels the command for the user and tells Claude when to load the skill automatically — if a user’s request matches the description, Claude may invoke the skill without being asked.

item.title: Subagents
item.description:
Subagents are child agents that Claude Code spawns via the Agent tool to handle focused tasks. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, scoped tool access, and independent permissions. When the subagent completes, it returns a single result message to the parent — only the relevant output, not the child’s full working context.

The purpose is twofold. Context isolation: the subagent’s file reads, searches, and intermediate reasoning do not consume the parent’s context, preventing long research or exploration from diluting the parent’s working memory. Parallelism: multiple subagents can run concurrently on independent tasks, and the parent can continue its own work while background subagents execute.

item.title: AGENTS.md
item.description:
AGENTS.md is a markdown file checked into a git repository that customizes how AI coding agents behave. It sits at the top of the conversation history, below the system prompt, and loads on every request.

Origin and adoption

The AGENTS.md convention started as a collaborative effort by Sourcegraph and is now maintained by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation. It has been adopted by OpenAI (Codex), Google (Gemini CLI, Jules), GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf, Aider, Zed, Warp, Roo Code, AMP, and others. It aims to replace vendor-specific files (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, COPILOT.md) with a single shared standard.

item.title: CLAUDE.md
item.description:
A CLAUDE.md file is a markdown document that Claude Code reads at the start of every conversation. It provides persistent, project-specific context that the agent cannot infer from code alone.

Load order

Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from four locations, additively:

  1. Global (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) — personal preferences, all projects
  2. Project root (./CLAUDE.md) — committed to version control, shared
  3. Subdirectory (path/to/dir/CLAUDE.md) — loaded lazily when Claude works in that directory
  4. User-specific (.claude/CLAUDE.md) — personal overrides, gitignored

Ancestor files load at startup. Subdirectory files load lazily when the agent touches files in that directory. Sibling directories never cross-load.

item.title: Progressive Disclosure in Agent Instructions
item.description:
Progressive disclosure is an information architecture principle applied to AI agent instruction files. The root instruction file (CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md) contains only universally applicable rules. Task-specific, domain-specific, or tool-specific instructions live in subdirectory files, skill documents, or referenced specifications.

Why it matters

Every token in a root instruction file loads on every request. Long root files create three problems:

  1. Budget pressure — instruction-following quality degrades as instruction count rises.
  2. Relevance dilution — instructions irrelevant to the current task compete for attention with relevant ones.
  3. Staleness risk — more content means more surface area for drift between instructions and actual project state.

The layering pattern

Layer File Loaded when Contains
Root CLAUDE.md (repo root) Every session Build commands, architectural constraints, what not to touch, pointers to key files
Subdirectory subdir/CLAUDE.md When working in that directory Local conventions, tool-specific setup, domain rules
Skill skills/*/SKILL.md When skill is invoked Detailed operational instructions for one task
Reference Specification or guide files When agent reads them on demand Full style guides, formalization protocols, domain specs

In this repository

The emsenn.net repository applies progressive disclosure across two layers:
